title: The Explanation “Jaya” Gave about Bonus!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: The front-page banner headline in the ruling party's newspaper and its supporting papers on 22 October 2013 was: "A Mother's Loving Gift of Rs 240 Crore for a Joyous Deepavali: 20 Per Cent Deepavali Bonus for Public-Sector Employees, Announces Chief Minister Jayalalithaa."

I could only laugh when I read it. We are told that Amma has lovingly gifted government employees a twenty per cent bonus so that they may celebrate Deepavali happily. The report reminded me of the past, and I could not avoid recalling how this same Jayalalithaa had criticised government employees and the payment of bonuses.
Speaking in the Assembly on 30 October 2002, Jayalalithaa said:

> They speak repeatedly about bonus. What is a bonus? How did it originate? The idea was that when an enterprise functioned well and earned a profit, it was not fair for the entire profit to go to its owners. Who earned that profit for the enterprise? Its employees. A bonus is therefore a gesture of goodwill through which owners give the workers who earned that profit a share of it. But how can it be fair to demand a bonus even when an enterprise operates at a loss?
We cannot forget that she spoke in this severely critical manner.
One could write an entire large book titled *Government Employees and Madam Jayalalithaa*. At this moment, when she has announced a bonus, let me recall a few matters.

In 1989, the DMK government appointed 13,000 educated unemployed young people as People's Welfare Workers at an honorarium of Rs 750 a month. As soon as Jayalalithaa became Chief Minister in 1991, she dismissed every one of them. When the DMK returned in 1996, I restored their employment and brought light back into their lives. When Jayalalithaa returned to office in 2011, she mercilessly dismissed them again.
To create further employment for young people, we appointed 10,000 road workers. When Jayalalithaa became Chief Minister again in 2001, she dismissed all 10,000 with a stone heart and sent them home.

What can people who have worked and supported their families for years do when employment suddenly disappears? Many died by suicide. Marriages already arranged were cancelled, leaving the women they were to marry helpless.

People's Welfare Worker Veerappan of Kameswaram village, Vairavankattalai, near Keezhaiyur in Nagapattinam district, consumed poison and died on 11 June 2001. After C. Kandasamy, a People's Welfare Worker in Oddanchatram union, Dindigul district, lost his job, his wife Prabha consumed poison and died the same day. On 12 June, Thilagar, who had worked in Natham union, also took his life, unable to bear the pain. The DMK gave each family Rs 25,000 in relief.

Ramamoorthy of Aruppukkottai stopped eating in grief after losing his job and died of starvation. Ilangovan of Tharangambadi in Nagapattinam district consumed poison. Kabilan of Mandapam in Ramanathapuram district starved to death after losing his job. Jayalalithaa's mercilessness turned such tragedies into a continuing story.

An authoritarian government order declared that striking public employees could be imprisoned for three years and fined Rs 5,000. It prohibited every kind of leave during protest days, ordered immediate submission of lists of absentees, and denied wages for those days. Under the Essential Services Maintenance Act, ESMA, the government arrested leaders of striking employees in 2002. It also introduced the Tamil Nadu Essential Services Maintenance Act, TESMA, as an emergency law and dismissed workers.

After seeing the dismissal list, the roll of those who lost their lives grew: headmistress Renuka Periyarasan near Peravurani in Thanjavur district; Kumarasamy of Anthiyur; assistant headmistress Sarojini of Kodumudi; Assistant Elementary Education Officer Alagammal; Sengodraj of Denkanikottai; Solaiappan of the Directorate of Collegiate Education; Pachaiyappan of Villivakkam; and Anjugam of Pullambadi.
Petitions were filed in the Madras High Court seeking cancellation of the dismissals and release of arrested employees. In the judgment delivered on 6 July 2003, the judge said:

> I gave the government two days to reach a good decision. It announced none and delayed the matter. The court merely asked that everyone be released, but the government failed even to do that. The employees have voluntarily offered to withdraw the strike. I therefore accept that and issue this order. The government's failure to act is unfortunate; its delay is deeply regrettable and painful. Dismissing public employees without a fair inquiry is wrong and unlawful. I release them all. Everyone arrested under Sections 4 and 5 of ESMA must be released immediately, and the Director General of Police must implement this at once. I stay every suspension and dismissal order. Everyone must return to work immediately. No new appointments may be made.

When government counsel asked for the order to be stayed for two days, the judge refused and said: "Just as people who had done nothing were awakened and arrested at midnight, release them this very midnight."

Rather than accept this judgment, the Jayalalithaa government went overnight to the residence of the Chief Justice of the Madras High Court, appealed, and obtained an interim stay of the order delivered that evening. The same Jayalalithaa who displayed such hostility and hatred towards public employees now wears a diversionary mask of affection and announces a Deepavali bonus.

As another cruelty, after dismissing employees and without even waiting for the judgment, the government announced recruitment of 15,500 replacements, creating great confusion. Government appointments should be made through examinations conducted by the Public Service Commission. Instead, candidates were selected through ruling-party recommendations. Then, in June 2004, the AIADMK government suddenly ordered all those employees removed. I issued a statement saying it was wrong to destroy the lives of people to whom a helping hand had been extended. Opposition leaders also condemned the removal. The government finally yielded and withdrew its order.
On 12 August 2003, the AIADMK government cancelled the recognition of more than 300 associations of public employees and teachers that had joined the strike.

Speaking on 24 July 2001, Jayalalithaa advanced the perverse argument that 70 per cent of total tax revenue went to public-employee salaries and 24 per cent to pensions, leaving only six per cent for development.

Until 2000-01, the DMK government gave public employees and teachers a Pongal gift. The AIADMK stopped it after taking office in 2001-02. It also refused the twenty per cent bonus the DMK had given transport workers, offering only the statutory minimum of 8.33 per cent. When repeated negotiations failed, transport workers from every union struck, buses stopped, and Tamil Nadu was paralysed.

When the DMK returned in 2006, we accepted the demand to restore the benefit. On 28 December 2006, I announced bonus, special bonus and Pongal gifts to encourage all public employees and teachers, at a cost of Rs 248.68 crore.

The annual performance incentive for employees of the Tamil Nadu State Transport Corporations, Tamil Nadu Transport Development Finance Corporation and Pallavan Transport Consultancy Services had also been withheld throughout the five AIADMK years from 2002 to 2006. After the DMK returned, it restored the incentive for Pongal. A total of Rs 7.007 crore was paid to 123,089 transport employees, enabling them to celebrate with their families.

The incentive was not confined to transport workers. It was extended to Tamil Nadu Electricity Board employees and workers in other public enterprises, including the Civil Supplies Corporation, Arasu Rubber Corporation, Tamil Nadu Forest Plantation Corporation, Tamil Nadu Tea Plantation Corporation, Housing Board, Milk Producers' Cooperative Federation and seventeen district milk unions, as well as cooperative-society employees.

After the DMK assumed office in 2006, contract and temporary workers and Group C and D employees under TASMAC were also granted a total twenty per cent bonus: 8.33 per cent bonus plus 11.67 per cent ex gratia.

The present government has therefore announced precisely the same twenty per cent bonus that the DMK government gave workers in Tamil Nadu's public enterprises. The DMK gave transport and other workers eighteen per cent in 1996, nineteen per cent in 1997, and twenty per cent in 1998, 1999 and 2000. At the time, the AIADMK organised strikes demanding twenty-five per cent and claiming twenty was insufficient. Now, in office, it announces only the same twenty per cent given by the DMK.
Even this announcement was made unilaterally, in the spirit of "all praise belongs to Amma," without respecting the working class by first inviting major trade-union representatives for talks.
When out of office, they say one thing. Once in power, they forget and conceal it and do another. That has become this government's custom, and the public-sector bonus issue proves it yet again.
With affection,
M. K.
24 October 2013
